activities_projects.title: Weighted Mean Investigation
activities_projects.objective: Understand how different weights affect the average
steps: Assume a student's final grade is 40% exams, 40% projects, and 20% homework
Calculate the grade for a student with scores: Exam=70, Project=90, Homework=100
Compare this 'weighted mean' to the simple arithmetic mean of the three scores
Explain why they differ
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Analyzes complex representative values used in real-world grading
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy of weighted calculation and comparison (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Outlier Impact Study
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the sensitivity of mean and median to extreme values
steps: Create a data set of 10 household incomes: nine at 30,000 and one at 5,00,000
Calculate the Mean and Median
Represent this data on a number line
Argue which value (Mean or Median) better represents the 'common ground' of the group
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Critically evaluates the reliability of different central tendencies
activities_projects.evaluation: Strength of the mathematical argument regarding outliers (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Advanced Double Bar Graph Interpretation
activities_projects.objective: Analyze trends and proportions in comparative data
materials: Double bar graph of Import/Export data
steps: Find a graph showing multi-year performance of two companies
Calculate the percentage increase or decrease for both companies over three years
Predict the next year's value based on the observed trend
Discuss the limitations of such predictions
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Uses data visualization for trend analysis and forecasting
activities_projects.evaluation: Depth of trend analysis and calculation accuracy (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Probability of Compound Events
activities_projects.objective: Calculate probability for two independent events
steps: Toss two coins simultaneously 40 times
Record outcomes: (H,H), (H,T), (T,H), (T,T)
Calculate the experimental probability of getting 'exactly one head'
Compare the result with the theoretical probability ($1/2$)
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Masters the concept of sample space and compound probability
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct recording of sample space and probability (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Backtracking the Mean
activities_projects.objective: Find a missing value using the given mean
steps: The mean of 5 numbers is 28. If one number is excluded, the mean becomes 25
Write an algebraic equation to find the excluded number
Solve for $x$ and verify the result
Create a similar problem involving 10 numbers and solve it
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Uses algebraic manipulation to solve data problems
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct algebraic steps and final answer (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Birthday Paradox (Mini Version)
steps: Collect the birth months of 20 random people
Find the frequency of each month
Calculate the probability that a person picked at random was born in a month with 31 days
Check if any two people share the same birth month and discuss the 'chance'
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Applies probability concepts to group demographics
activities_projects.evaluation: Accurate frequency distribution and probability (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Scale Manipulation in Graphs
activities_projects.objective: Understand how graph scales can misrepresent data
steps: Draw a bar graph for the data [A=10, B=12] using a scale starting at 0
Draw the same data using a scale starting at 8 (a 'broken' axis)
Explain how the second graph makes the difference between A and B look much larger
Discuss why this can be misleading in advertisements
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Develops critical thinking towards visual data representation
activities_projects.evaluation: Clarity of the two graphs and the ethical discussion (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Mean of Grouped Data (Simplified)
activities_projects.objective: Estimate the mean of data in intervals
materials: Frequency table from previous Medium activity
steps: Find the 'mid-point' of each age interval (e.g., for 10-20, mid-point is 15)
Multiply each mid-point by its frequency
Sum these products and divide by the total frequency
Compare this estimated mean with the actual mean of the raw data
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Introduces the concept of group-mean calculation
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct application of the $\frac{\sum f \cdot x}{\sum f}$ logic (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Non-Numerical Data Handling
activities_projects.objective: Determine central tendency for categorical data
steps: Collect blood group data (A, B, AB, O) from 20 people
Explain why the 'Mean' cannot be calculated for this data
Identify the Mode and discuss why it is the only useful representative value here
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Differentiates between numerical and categorical data analysis
activities_projects.evaluation: Logical explanation of data types and mode (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Data Scientist’s Report
activities_projects.objective: Synthesize all data handling concepts into a project
steps: Conduct a survey on 'Hours of sleep vs. Academic scores' for 10 students
Represent the data using a double bar graph
Calculate the Range, Mean, and Median for both variables
Write a 100-word conclusion on whether 'common ground' exists between the two variables
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Combines collection, organization, representation, and interpretation of data
activities_projects.evaluation: Completeness and professionalism of the final report (10 marks)
